speaker: ALEX
text: Welcome back. This time, we're building a practical reference in audio form: the accessibility standards that show up when you use GitHub, review pull requests, write docs, or contribute code to the web.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So this is the episode for the moment when somebody comments, "This may fail WCAG 1.4.3," and you think, I know that matters, but I need help decoding it.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Think of this as a working guide, not a memorization exercise. It's a companion to open source collaboration and also very relevant when you work in VS Code or inspect web interfaces. And if you want the canonical standards, the authoritative references are the W3C documents for WCAG 2.2 and WAI-ARIA 1.2.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we dive in, can we set expectations? Are learners supposed to memorize criterion numbers?

speaker: ALEX
text: No. You should recognize the big ideas, know the common trouble spots, and know where to look things up. This appendix is a lookup reference, and this episode turns that into a conversation you can carry into reviews and bug reports.

speaker: ALEX
text: Let's orient the map. The material moves through the four WCAG principles, conformance levels, commonly encountered success criteria, ARIA basics, landmark roles, common ARIA patterns, how standards apply to GitHub contributions, testing, a pull request checklist, and official references.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's helpful, because accessibility standards can feel like a giant wall of acronyms unless you know the structure.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. And the structure starts with WCAG.

speaker: ALEX
text: WCAG stands for Web Content Accessibility Guidelines. Version 2.2 is organized around four principles often remembered with the mnemonic POUR: Perceivable, Operable, Understandable, and Robust.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's make each one concrete.

speaker: ALEX
text: Perceivable means information must be available in ways users can actually perceive. If an image has no alt text, a screen reader user may get nothing meaningful. That's a perceivability failure.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the content exists visually, but not accessibly.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Operable means people must be able to use the interface. If a control only works on hover, keyboard users may be blocked. That's an operability failure.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Understandable sounds like both wording and behavior.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. If a form says only "Invalid input" but never explains what is wrong, the system is not understandable enough. Users need clear labels, instructions, and error explanations.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And Robust?

speaker: ALEX
text: Robust means content has to work with assistive technologies and across different user agents. A custom widget with JavaScript but no proper semantics may leave a screen reader unable to identify what it is, what state it's in, or how to interact with it.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So every WCAG success criterion belongs to one of those four buckets.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. That's the high-level index for everything else.

speaker: JAMIE
text: The learning cards in the source also had audience-specific advice. Can we fold that in?

speaker: ALEX
text: Absolutely. For screen reader users: remember POUR as your fast review lens, know that many projects target WCAG 2.2 AA, and when someone cites a number like 1.4.3, the WCAG quick reference is searchable by criterion number. For low-vision users: contrast criteria like 1.4.3 for text contrast and 1.4.11 for non-text contrast are especially relevant day to day. If tables are hard to read in plain Markdown, use larger editor text or Markdown preview. And when checking contrast with a tool like WebAIM's contrast checker, use your usual zoom level so you stay in your normal workflow. For sighted users: the quick mental shortcut is to connect each principle to a typical failure. Also, pay attention to conformance levels in tables, because AA is usually the practical target. And the ARIA Authoring Practices Guide is worth bookmarking for real pattern examples.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That leads nicely to conformance levels. What do A, AA, and AAA really mean in practice?

speaker: ALEX
text: Level A is the baseline. It removes the most severe barriers and is often the minimum legal floor in many contexts. Level AA goes further and removes significant barriers; it's the common compliance target for WCAG 2.2, for many enterprise and government contexts, and it aligns with GitHub's accessibility commitments. Level AAA is the highest level, but it is not generally required across the board and is applied where feasible.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So if I'm contributing to an open source web project and I want to use the standard most reviewers will recognize, I should usually reference WCAG 2.2 AA.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. That is the safest default unless the project says otherwise.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now let's cover the success criteria you are most likely to encounter in web contributions.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And maybe translate them into things you can notice in a pull request.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: ALEX
text: Under Perceivable, one of the biggest is 1.1.1 Non-text Content. Images, icons, and other non-text content need text alternatives, whether that's alt text, an accessible name, or another appropriate text equivalent.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So a meaningful icon-only button with no label is a problem.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Next is 1.3.1 Info and Relationships. If structure is communicated visually with headings, lists, or tables, that structure also needs to exist programmatically.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Meaning you can't just make text look like a heading with bold styling.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Use real headings. Then 1.3.2 Meaningful Sequence: the reading order should still make sense, even if CSS is removed or layout shifts. That matters because assistive tech often follows DOM order, not just visual layout.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And 1.3.3 Sensory Characteristics says don't rely only on things like "click the green button on the right."

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Instructions should not depend solely on color, shape, sound, or position. 1.4.1 Use of Color continues that theme: color alone cannot carry the information.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Then the contrast criteria.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. 1.4.3 Contrast Minimum requires at least a 4.5 to 1 contrast ratio for normal text, or 3 to 1 for large text. 1.4.4 Resize Text means text can be resized to 200 percent without losing content or functionality. 1.4.10 Reflow means content should work at 400 percent zoom without forcing horizontal scrolling in typical cases. And 1.4.11 Non-text Contrast requires UI components and graphical objects to meet at least 3 to 1 contrast.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That one gets missed a lot, right? People think only body text contrast matters.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Focus indicators, buttons, form boundaries, icons, and other UI elements matter too.

speaker: ALEX
text: Under Operable, start with 2.1.1 Keyboard. All functionality must be available by keyboard alone.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Which means if I can only trigger a control with a mouse, that's a failure.

speaker: ALEX
text: Correct. Then 2.1.2 No Keyboard Trap: once focus enters a component, the user must be able to move focus away. A modal, menu, or custom widget cannot trap someone indefinitely.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And 2.4.1 Bypass Blocks is where skip links come in.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Repeated navigation should be skippable. 2.4.2 Page Titled means pages need descriptive titles. 2.4.3 Focus Order means keyboard focus should proceed logically. 2.4.4 Link Purpose means link text should say where it goes or what it does.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So "click here" and "read more" are often weak if they're ambiguous out of context.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Then 2.4.6 Headings and Labels says headings and labels should describe topic or purpose. 2.4.7 Focus Visible requires a visible focus indicator for keyboard users. 2.4.11 Focus Appearance, which is new in WCAG 2.2, raises the bar by requiring minimum size and contrast for that focus indicator.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's a good example of standards evolving from "there is some focus" to "the focus has to be meaningfully perceivable."

speaker: ALEX
text: Well put. And 2.5.3 Label in Name matters especially for speech input and consistency. If a control visibly says "Submit order," the accessible name should contain that visible text, not something unrelated like "Confirm purchase now."

speaker: ALEX
text: Under Understandable, 3.1.1 Language of Page says the page language must be identified, usually with the HTML lang attribute. 3.2.1 On Focus means merely receiving focus should not trigger a surprising context change. 3.2.2 On Input says changing a setting should not unexpectedly launch something or move the user somewhere else unless they were warned.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So just tabbing into a field shouldn't open a new page, and selecting an option shouldn't suddenly submit a form without clear expectation.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Then 3.3.1 Error Identification says input errors need to be identified and described in text. 3.3.2 Labels or Instructions means forms need labels or instructions where input is required. And 3.3.3 Error Suggestion says that when possible, users should be told how to correct the issue, not merely that something went wrong.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's a subtle but important distinction: "invalid input" versus "password must be at least twelve characters."

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Good accessible design reduces guesswork.

speaker: ALEX
text: Under Robust, 4.1.1 Parsing means markup should not contain errors that break assistive technology interpretation. 4.1.2 Name, Role, Value says UI components need accessible names, semantic roles, and states that can be determined programmatically. And 4.1.3 Status Messages says updates like "item added" or "saved successfully" should be announced without moving focus.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That last one connects directly to live regions and polite announcements.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly, and we'll get there shortly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now for the other big acronym: ARIA.

speaker: ALEX
text: ARIA stands for Accessible Rich Internet Applications. It helps when native HTML cannot fully express a complex interface. But it's not a replacement for semantic HTML. It fills gaps.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the source breaks ARIA into three categories.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Roles say what something is: button, dialog, tab. States describe changing conditions: expanded, checked, disabled. Properties describe characteristics or relationships: labels, descriptions, requiredness, and so on.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Can you give quick examples?

speaker: ALEX
text: Sure. A custom trigger might use role equals button. An accordion header may expose aria-expanded true or false. A close control might use aria-label equals Close dialog. An input may point to helper text with aria-describedby.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What are the main rules people should remember?

speaker: ALEX
text: Three big ones. First: do not use ARIA when native HTML already works. If you can use a real button, use a real button. Second: if you do create an interactive ARIA widget, it must be keyboard operable. A div with role button is not enough; you also need keyboard handling, focusability, and expected behavior. Third: ARIA affects the accessibility tree, not appearance or behavior. It changes what assistive technologies can understand, but it does not magically add visuals or interaction logic.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So ARIA can describe, but not fully build.

speaker: ALEX
text: Perfectly said.

speaker: ALEX
text: A particularly important ARIA topic is landmark roles. These let screen reader users jump quickly among major page regions.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And that's very relevant on large sites like GitHub.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Common landmarks include banner for a site-wide header, navigation for nav links, main for primary page content, complementary for supporting side content, contentinfo for footer-like site information, search for search regions, and form when a form region needs landmark semantics.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And often there are native HTML equivalents, right?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Header, nav, main, aside, footer, search-related structures, and forms often provide or imply these semantics depending on usage. The broader lesson is: prefer semantic HTML first, and understand landmarks as navigation points for assistive tech.

speaker: JAMIE
text: The source also notes practical navigation commands.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Screen reader users often jump by landmarks using commands like D in NVDA or JAWS, or the Rotor in VoiceOver. That's one reason clear page regions matter so much.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's make ARIA more concrete with patterns people actually implement.

speaker: ALEX
text: Good idea. One pattern is the accessible button when a real button cannot be used.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Which is not the preferred choice.

speaker: ALEX
text: Correct. Preferred is always the real button element. But if you inherit a difficult codebase or truly cannot use button, the substitute needs at minimum button semantics, keyboard focus, and activation with Enter and Space. It also needs a clear accessible name.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the lesson is less "here's a cool div trick" and more "if you must do this, you inherit several responsibilities."

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: ALEX
text: Another pattern is an accessible form field with an error. A form control needs a proper label, and if there is an error, that error should be described in text, associated with the field, and ideally exposed in a way assistive technologies can announce. If the field is invalid, state should be programmatically represented, often with something like aria-invalid, plus a descriptive error message connected through an accessible relationship such as aria-describedby.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And that matches the WCAG requirements we talked about: error identification, labels or instructions, and error suggestions.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly right.

speaker: ALEX
text: Then there are live region status announcements. This is where 4.1.3 Status Messages often shows up. When something changes dynamically, like "settings saved" or "issue created," users of assistive tech should be informed without having focus yanked away. Live regions provide that channel.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Usually with polite announcements unless it's urgent.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Use urgency carefully. The goal is to inform, not overwhelm.

speaker: ALEX
text: And finally, expandable sections, like accordions or disclosure widgets. The trigger should communicate whether the section is expanded or collapsed, usually through an expandable state, and the control should be keyboard operable, clearly labeled, and logically connected to the content it reveals.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Which is why a native details and summary pattern can sometimes be the best answer.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Native elements reduce the amount you have to recreate.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now let's bring this back to GitHub contributions, because that's where learners need to apply the standards.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. There are three recurring situations here: filing an accessibility bug, reviewing a pull request for accessibility, and writing accessible documentation.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Start with bug reports.

speaker: ALEX
text: When filing an accessibility bug, be specific and standard-aware. Describe the user impact, the exact steps to reproduce, the environment if relevant, and the observed behavior. Then, where possible, connect it to a WCAG criterion. For example, instead of saying "the form is inaccessible," say "the submit control has no accessible name, which affects screen reader users and may fail WCAG 4.1.2 Name, Role, Value."

speaker: JAMIE
text: That gives maintainers something actionable rather than just alarming.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. If it's a contrast issue, cite the ratio and likely criterion. If it's keyboard access, mention the exact key path that fails. If it's a missing status announcement, explain what happened and what was not announced.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How about pull request review?

speaker: ALEX
text: Reviewing a PR for accessibility means checking the common standards we covered against the actual user experience. Ask: Are names, labels, and roles present? Is everything keyboard operable? Is focus visible and logical? Are headings meaningful? Does link text make sense? Do errors identify the problem and suggest correction? Are dynamic updates announced? Does zoom or reflow break the interface? Is color doing too much work by itself?

speaker: JAMIE
text: So you can review both code and behavior.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Sometimes the markup looks fine until you actually tab through it or use a screen reader.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And writing accessible documentation?

speaker: ALEX
text: That matters more than many contributors realize. Use real heading structure, descriptive link text, meaningful alt text for informative images, and tables only when data is truly tabular. Avoid instructions based only on color or screen position. If a process depends on visuals, describe the nonvisual cues too. And when writing for GitHub issues, READMEs, or contributor guides, clarity is accessibility.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That includes administrative content too, right? Not just app UI.

speaker: ALEX
text: Absolutely. Documentation, checklists, issue templates, contributing guides, and changelog notes are all part of an accessible workflow.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Testing is the other huge theme. The appendix makes a strong point that automated tools only catch part of the problem.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Automated tools are useful, but they typically catch only about thirty to forty percent of issues. They're great for finding missing alt text, some contrast failures, certain form labeling problems, missing landmarks, some ARIA misuse, and structural issues.

speaker: JAMIE
text: But they can't tell the whole story.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. They usually cannot judge whether alt text is meaningful, whether focus order truly makes sense, whether instructions are understandable, whether the reading order is coherent, whether a custom interaction is usable, or whether documentation is actually clear.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So manual testing catches the rest.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Manual testing should include keyboard-only testing, checking focus visibility and order, zoom and reflow testing, reviewing with a screen reader when possible, inspecting headings and landmarks, confirming link purpose, and trying forms with real error states.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Can we include the learning-card style guidance here too?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. For testing against standards, the mindset is: use automation first for broad detection, then manual testing for human judgment and interaction quality. If you're a screen reader user, use landmarks, headings, and form navigation commands to evaluate structure efficiently. If you're a low-vision tester, zoom and contrast checks are essential, not optional. If you're a sighted keyboard tester, pay special attention to visible focus, logical tab order, and whether interactive elements expose their purpose without relying on a mouse.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now let's compress that into a fast pull request checklist.

speaker: JAMIE
text: The kind of thing you can mentally run before approving.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. In a PR, check: Are images and icon-only controls given meaningful text alternatives? Are headings, lists, and tables semantically correct? Does the reading order make sense? Are instructions independent of color and position? Is text contrast sufficient, and do UI elements meet non-text contrast needs? Can everything be used with a keyboard? Is there any keyboard trap? Is focus order logical and focus indication clearly visible? Do page titles, headings, labels, and links describe purpose well? Does the accessible name contain the visible label where needed? Are language, labels, instructions, and error messages present and clear? Do error messages identify the issue and suggest a fix? Do components expose name, role, and value correctly? Are status messages announced without stealing focus? If ARIA is used, is it necessary, and is the widget fully keyboard operable?

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's a great practical translation of the standards into review behavior.

speaker: ALEX
text: That's the goal. Standards should guide contribution decisions, not just sit in a reference document.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we close, what should learners treat as the official source of truth?

speaker: ALEX
text: For WCAG, use the W3C WCAG 2.2 specification and the W3C WCAG overview and quick reference materials. For ARIA, use WAI-ARIA 1.2 and the WAI-ARIA Authoring Practices Guide. For implementation help and examples, WAI tutorials are excellent. And for platform-specific context, GitHub Docs and the GitHub Changelog can help you track product behavior and documentation changes.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So if a project debate starts, "I read a blog post that saidâ€¦," you can ground the conversation in W3C and primary documentation.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. The appendix also included an authoritative source map by section, and the big idea there is simple: use official standards and official implementation guides whenever possible.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let me try a final summary without turning this into a shallow recap. WCAG gives us the four-part mental model: Perceivable, Operable, Understandable, Robust. Most real-world contribution work targets WCAG 2.2 AA. The success criteria we'll see most often involve text alternatives, semantic structure, contrast, zoom, keyboard access, focus behavior, clear labels and errors, and programmatic name-role-value support. ARIA helps with complex widgets, but native HTML is preferred. Landmarks and common patterns matter because they shape real navigation for assistive tech. And accessibility testing has to combine automation with manual checks.

speaker: ALEX
text: That's excellent. And I'd add one final contributor mindset: when you file issues, review pull requests, or write documentation, try to name both the user impact and the likely standard involved. That combination makes you much more effective in collaborative accessibility work.

speaker: JAMIE
text: In other words, not just "this is broken," but "this blocks keyboard users," or "this status change is not announced," or "this may fail WCAG 4.1.3."

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Precision helps teams fix real barriers.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And it helps learners grow from "I know accessibility matters" to "I can participate in accessibility review with confidence."

speaker: ALEX
text: Well said.

speaker: ALEX
text: That's it for this reference conversation. Keep the standards close, use them as tools rather than trivia, and let them shape how you contribute on GitHub and beyond.
